segments.text: World News Today, brought to you by Admiral Corporation, in behalf of Admiral Distributors
and Dealers all over America and in many foreign lands.
By shortwave broadcast direct from important overseas stations and leading news centers
in our own country, CBS reporters are waiting to bring you first-hand news from the world's
political and battlefronts.
Now here's Douglas Edwards.
Two American armies were on the attack today.
First Army forces are threatening the German city of Oken within
servlement.
And a front dispatch reports the General Patton's Third Army has moved six
miles forward between Metz and Nasi.
The pre-Yugaslav radio says Russian and Yugoslav forces have begun the
final attack on Belgrade.
In Greece, British troops are nearing the outskirts of Karinth.
In Italy, Americans were 12 miles from Bologna.
And early this morning, Wendell Wilkie died in a New York hospital.
Those are the principal developments.
Now for the tales of the news from the western front, Admiral takes you to Belgium, Richard C.
Hotelette reporting.
The American First Army's chapter is now full-length behind the city of Arthur.
North and east of it are empty as pushing together.

In the first hours of the attack, the foot of the tower was well out in Hyder against only

They'll only comparatively fuel troops in it, and out of a peacetime population of 160,000,
we don't think they'll have more than about 10,000 hiding in the cellar.
I've looked into the city for one of our forward observations for it.
It's very valuable damage, and its main value towards will be the network of good roads that span out from it.
There's no sign the Germans will make any gesture to tempt the hold on them.
The matter of fact, they may very well try to pull out tonight while they still have a chance,

And for supplementary information, admiral takes you now to London, Larry Lesur reporting.
With the piercing of the Ziegfried Line, defenses north of Aachen, accomplished by the American
First Army, a new attack has been started further south, this time by General Patron's Third
Army.
The Third Army offensive began at all this morning on the 20-mile front between the French
Fortress cities of Metz and Nalsee, where the Germans hold a shallow salient, but a well-defendant
one.
The Third Army Doe boys jumped up through a smoke screen after the German positions had
been heavily bombarded, and latest reports say we've cleared at least eight towns and
control important hills after denting the German defenses for a gain of six miles.
Up in Western Holland, the First Canadian Army is fighting bitterly in their bridgehead across
the Leopold Canal.
This deeply contested bridgehead still consists of two Canadian pockets not yet linked up.
Powerful German artillery and mortar fire has delayed the building of a bridge across
the canal.
These German troops, cornered on the shores of the Shelter Estuary, are good troops and
they are fighting back well.
Clearing up this pocket will not be a walkover.
Further east, the Canadian advanced northward from Antwerp is going more quickly.
The Canadians there on the point of cutting the German escape route from the Shelter Islands
off the Dutch coast. The causeway leading to the Dutch mainland is already on the Canadian
artillery fire. Once this is cut, the Germans in the half-footed island of Valserin and
the Shelgepocket will have little chance of escaping. And things were made worse for them
today, by yesterday's RAF raid which smashed the Dijk East of Flashing and poured another
deluge of seawater across Valserin Island. To the forest south of the front, the Germans
have been counter-attacking heavily against the American seventh eye east of
Epinar. The Germans are also hanging on just north of the Belford gap. That is
at the town of Littilo. A big road center with direct communications to the
famous Belford gap and the lower Ryan Valley. Yesterday the Germans tried to
head off the Americans in circling the town but were beaten off. This is tough

Now here once again is Douglas Edwards.
And for a summary of the latest developments in Italy and the Peloponnesis, Admiral takes
you to CVS Rome, Winston Burdett reporting.
This is the critical season on the Italian front.
The first smells have fallen in the mountains, the rains continue.
In the past week, the Americans have edged five miles closer to Bologna.
They have 13 miles to go.
The next two or three weeks made aside,
whether our enemies here will join in the general assault
on Germany's own frontiers,
or will spend another winter fighting on Italian soil.
Here in Rome, and our economic chiefs are worried
about the coming months in Italy.
About the 22 million Italians in America,
when we may have to feed this winter.
The first reaction here to President Roosevelt's statement
on increased aid to Italy came from the acting chief
of the Army of Control Commission's economic section,
Anthony Antelina.
The president announced that 150,000 pounds of weed
and fur were scheduled for shipment here.
Regardless of that shipment, Mr. Antelini says,
the Allied Control Commission would have
to face the grave question of reducing
the red ration scale in Italy.
If our armies are moving north,
find that the Germans have largely destroyed
or taken away the grain hovers to the full valley.
Repositive statement did not say when the Green Shipments for Eiffelays should arrive.
According to Mr. Andolini, basic food requirements demand the importation of 100,000 times of

Allied economic chiefs believe we should be able to get through the winter if the front
remains where it is.
But if the imports do not come, and if unmoving north we find extensive food and flooding in the
that a general blood ration cut may be necessary.
Advancing into Northern Italy, the Allies will face imponderables of a very serious nature.
How much of Italy's hardest remains?
How much of Italy's industry have the Germans destroyed or dismantled?
How many hundreds of thousands of unemployed will not be in the industrial north?
The Lord's center of the country, where people are quicker to react,
the ludiclaim are awake and around.
We know that German experts are going through the fatagian industry with a fine-truve code.
Stripping it up all they can use in Germany, scientifically wrecking the rest.
All our reports indicate that we will find in northern Italy, social chaos without power,
even in this idle, hungry and war-ravaged country.
Winston-Bredett has reported from Rome.
Now for the tales of the vast Russian campaigns in the east, Admiral takes you to CBS Moscow,
Leigh White of the Chicago Daily News Foreign Service reporting.
In Hungary, the Soviet offensive is progressing steadily according to reports from
Russian correspondents. German Hungarian resistance is pretty stiff,
but not as stiff as it might have been. The consensus is that the Hungarians
are all their honoured love of battle has not been fighting with any great enthusiasm.
The Chander Shag, the Hungarian John Dynamite, has been ordered to guard the rear and assure
that any soldiers will retreat.
As for the Germans in Hungary, Soviet correspondents comment on the large proportion of older
men among the prisoners among the prisoners, and the surprising number of them who were
glasses.
The new work of such German troops in the Soviet press is super-total soldiers.
have been thrown into the lines after the completion of Dr. Greville's so-called total mobilization.
A good idea of what the war has meant to the devastated farmlands of the Soviet Union
was contained in a letter made public today addressed to Mauritius Stirling from the agricultural
workers of the Kiev region in Ukraine. The letter states that more than half the tractors in
this area were destroyed or stolen by the Germans. Squares of thousands of people are dead
and other scores of thousands of men are still fighting at the front.
All but 8% of the oxygen in this area have disappeared, and only 39% of the horses remain.
The result is that instead of using fractures or even horses for plowing and harrowing this year,
many collective farms in the camp area had to use their milk cows for the purpose.
The scarcity of manly such that most of the farm work is not being run by women,
And the scarcity of even cows is such that large tractors of land have had to be worked
by woman power exclusively.
There are still some tractors left, of course, but 90% of them are now being operated by
women.
Formerly most tractors were men.
When the wheat was harvested last month, there were so little machinery available that 70%
of it had to be cut by hand with size, and the people who wielded the size were also
women.
In spite of the devastation of this part of the Ukraine, however, the collective farms
have already embarked on an ambitious program of reconstruction.
Next to tractors and other agricultural machinery, the project of the highest priority is housing.
Building materials are scarce in this earthy region, where there's little stone or clay.
And soil plan is being devised to produce lightweight building blocks from the pulp left
over after the juice has been extracted from sugar beads.
And returning now to New York, an offer a summary of the European news reaching Spain Admiral
takes you to CBS Madrid, Glen Stadler reporting.
Aspirin, the remedy for headaches is itself causing a temporary headache here in Spain
as the first cold blast of approaching winter sweep off the snow-capped mountains, lowering
the temperature to freezing, even down and usually bombing Seville.
A very limited supply has now come from Germany, and only by plane, stocks of pure aspirin
are practically exhausted.
Gregas already have dug deeply into the sun and the half reserve, where it got around
with the shortage and many of Spain's 20 million regular aspirin users made runs on
tomathe.
The stores unable to get stocks from the state authorized distributing agencies had duration
fills to one small tube for customers.
Fain, like most of the other countries in Europe,
had defended almost entirely on shipment from Germany,
which was Europe's biggest aspirin producer.

And each type that costs the equivalent of two American cents each one.
This is one starter in the grid, returning you to Admiral and New York.
In a Pacific war, our planes are still hammering at Jap bases from the curls in the north
to the southern Philippines.
West Pacific bombers struck at Zamboanga in the Southwest in Philippines, and they also
bombed airfields, docks, and shipping, and waters west of Dutch New Guinea.
In the corals, as Admiral Nimitz, our bombers thank a cargo ship.
In the Palau Islands, our tanks and artillery have made new progress against the Japs and
the Caves who bloody knows which, but it's still a slow and very difficult job.
Admiral Nimitz reports that so far over 12,000 Japs have been killed in the Palau campaign
we have captured 224.
Now for a discussion of the Russian situation,
here is Columbia's military analyst,
Major George Fielding Elliott.
Both on the north and the south,
the developments on the Russian front
are of considerable military importance.
The Russians have now gained possession
of the greater part of the Estonian island of Poso
or Salama, which when completed,
will give the Russian fleet full access
to the Baltic Sea.
South of Riga, the Russians are driving westward
to the Baltic shore.
this operation will is fully successful.
Not only can Spur the ports of Li Bao and Vindao from German-Irussian possession thus
being the Russian naval influence well down the Baltic and pushing the German naval power
back to Kenny's birth, but what is probably more important, it will bring the Russian
armies up to the Neiman River, the north-eastern frontier of East Prussia, and will therefore expose
that German province to attack on three sides.
The most important Russian gains, however, are in Hungary and Yugoslavia.
Very strong Russian forces are advancing northwestward into the Hungarian plain, driving toward
the capital, Budapest.
At last, before the Russians were only 83 miles from that city, where German and Hungarian
resistance are, apparently, growing weaker all along the front.
The Hungarian army has only eight good divisions, filled up on the framework of divisions
which part alongside the Germans in Russia.
perhaps eight additional divisions which have been recently raised and are deficient in
painting and equipment. For the most part the Hungarian army has old Czechoslovak tanks and guns
captured by the Germans in 1939 and transferred to the Hungarians. These are now obsolete and spare
parts for them are difficult to get. This may impart a count for the weakening of the Hungarian
resistance. In Yugoslavia, the Russians have joined the Marshall Tito forces in the Siege of Belgrade
and another Russian column is driving north toward Hungary.
So a third Russian force coming through Hungary has established two bridge-head
across the Danube, according to a German radio report
picked up by Columbia's short-wave listing station but not yet
confirmed from any Allied source.
If Bellgrade falls, the main escape route for the German troops in Greece
and southern Yugoslavia will have been cut.
This will be a hard blow to the Germans for every train German soldier is now
desperately needed for the defense of the Reich itself.
But even harder will be the blow if Budapest is taken, for this will threaten the opening
of a new southern front, which the Germans will be hard-driven to defend.
The Hungarian plane leads the Russian Army's northward toward the Moravian Gateway between
the Bohemian Health and the Carpathian Mountains, the historic opening into Germany from the
south.
It may prove that the roundabout route through Budapest is after all one of the shortcuts
to Berlin.
Now here again, it's that with Edwards.
A marine combat correspondent from Los Angeles
has just returned from the Battle of Calalue.
For his story, Admiral takes you to CVS Washington,
Don Pryor reporting.
I'd like to have you meet one of the first two Marines
to come back to the United States from Pelleleau
where the first marine division has been fighting
one of the bloodiest battles of the war for the last 23 days.
He's Sergeant Alvin G. Flanagan of Los Angeles.
Al hit the beach at Pelleleau on D-Day,
just 40 minutes after H hour. He went in on an M track with a microphone and a
film recorder. He stayed there for 12 days fighting and recording his impressions
of the battle as he went along. But he's here in person now. So I'll tell us
what it was like with you. To put it mildly it was a surprise to most of us. The
island is only two but five miles and for several days the Navy had blasted it
with big guns, aerial rockets and bombs and everything in the book. And just
Before each hour, the island looked as though Tojo's fireworks factory had received a direct
hit, and then we started in.
And even up to that time, we thought it was going to be a cinch.
The first two or three waves made it, but by that time, the Japs had claimed out of their
coral dugouts and set up their mortars.
And from then on, for the next week, anything that approached the beach was a mortar target,
and the Japs are artists with mortars.
Well, did you do any recording on the way in?
Or yes, I started in very blatantly, just as if I were back here broadcasting a football
game.
out was a veteran radio man in the States before he joined the Marines two years ago.
But I wasn't nonchalant for a long about it or what happened. About a thousand yards off
shore when we started through the reef, Jap mortars began dropping all around. Big eight inch
winds, the largest ones we'd ever seen. Just off the beach a Jap pillbox in a jungle thicket
opened up on us with a machine gun and hit five of our men. Lieutenant Colonel Walt sent in a
demolition our fit to knock it off.
While they circled around to attack from the beach side, private first-class
think of Cherry Tree, Pennsylvania, and I went into the thicket about 25 yards from the beach.
I was sitting there with a mic recording the work of the demolition crew when the
Jap ran out of the pillbox on our side right in front of us.
He was about 10 or 15 feet away with a machine gun cradled in his arms, pointed straight at us.
Think yelled and we both fired. The Jap was about a second too late.
Well, I'm glad of that, and so am I.
Now, in an operation like that, what are the biggest obstacles you have to overcome?
Aside from the japs, water was the chief problem.
For the first two days, we had to dig for it.
And because of the mortar fire, food was hard to get ashore.
I had a chocolate bar and a can of cheese in two and a half days.
And some of the guys in the front lines didn't have that much.
But beyond those obvious things, one of the worst obstacles has always scuttled by.
Well, how's that?
While rumors fly as fast and as thick as bullets, and you're worked up to such a pits that you'll believe anything,
D-Day plus two of us were pinned down in the shell hole in the middle of the air strip by sniper and mortar fire.
In the midst of all that, a voice came in on my walkie-talkie, identified himself with the right code word,
and told me that Germany had surrendered, and that MacArthur had invaded the Philippines.
We all believed that for about two days, and everybody had visions of going home pretty soon.
You can imagine what effect that had on most of this.
By the way, this wasn't Alice's first invasion.
You were on New Britain, weren't you?
Yes, I was, but not in the original landings, however.
At the time I got there, we only had job bombers to worry about.
But a lot of the guys in the first division have been through everything out there
since the original invasion of Water Canal.
Some of them have been there for 28 or 30 months.
And you'll never find a better bunch of guys to do Park Soul time with anywhere.
Well, I hope pretty soon you can spend time with them here at home.
I return you now to Admiral and New York.
The death of Wendell Wilkie in New York this morning brought this comment from President
Roosevelt.
The nation will long remember Wendell Wilkie as the fourth-right American, earnest, honest,
whole soul.
He also had tremendous courage.
This courage which was his dominating trait prompted him more than once to stand alone,
and to challenge the wisdom of councils taken by powerful interests within his own party.
In this hour of grave crisis, the country loses a great citizen through his untimely
death. Governor Joy, the Republican presidential nominee, described Wilkie as one of the great
men of our time. Joy said, his death will be mourned throughout the world. A forceful
and courageous man, he made a notable contribution to the political and philosophical discussions
of these critical times. His passing is a loss not only to the Republican Party, but to
all of us Americans of both parties who shared his faith for a world peace. And now, once
again, here is Warren Sweeney with a message from Admiral.
How better lot of your friends collect their favorite music on recordings, don't they?
Perhaps you're going to collect them too after the war when radio phonographs will again be available?
Yes, it's a great thrill for music lovers to select just the program they want
and hear it perfectly played with life-like reproduction on an Admiral Radio Phonograph, for example.

that it will set a new high in musical enjoyment.

From this vast experience in engineering and production,
plus continuous research has come many new ideas
for your better appreciation of your recorded favorites.
Being leaders in radio, radar, communications equipment,

some brand new ideas and radio too, so if you want a radio photograph with the best of

The nation's paper shortage grows more acute every day.
Each of us can help overcome this shortage by conserving every ounce of paper possible
and by salvaging every kind of waste paper.
Paper is essential to our war effort in the protection of food and equipment going to our
fighting men.
Let's all save all the paper we can.
World News Today is brought to you each Sunday at this hour by Admiral Corporation,
